name: OIDC Support
description: Add an OpenID Connect layer (ID tokens, UserInfo, discovery, JWKS) on top of the existing OAuth 2.0 authorization code grant.
why: Most teams searching for a TypeScript OAuth2 server actually want OIDC — they just don't know the distinction. Today the library answers OAuth questions but not authentication questions. Without ID tokens, a UserInfo endpoint, discovery, and a JWKS endpoint, it cannot be a relying party's login provider, which is what the majority of real-world auth integrations need.
outcome: A consumer can enable OIDC by supplying an asymmetric signing key and an OIDC config block. Relying parties can run the PKCE authorization code flow with scope=openid, receive a verifiable ID token, fetch user claims from UserInfo, and auto-configure via the discovery and JWKS endpoints. Non-OIDC consumers see zero behavior change.
in_scope: ID token issuance in the authorization code flow when the openid scope is granted
UserInfo endpoint returning scope-derived claims for a presented access token
JWKS endpoint publishing the public signing key
Asymmetric (RS256/ES256) token signing with a consumer-supplied keypair
nonce capture and propagation into the ID token
Standard OIDC scope-to-claim mapping and filtering
getUserClaims and optional getIdTokenClaims consumer hooks
out_of_scope: Implicit and hybrid flows — response_type=code only
FAPI 2.0 profiles, the claims request parameter, JAR (request/request_uri), and response_mode beyond query
Pairwise subject identifiers — public subject type only
ID token issuance on the refresh_token grant
Multi-key rotation machinery — kid headers and JWKS-as-array keep it a future additive change
Runtime key generation — keypair lifecycle is the consumer's responsibility
Formal OpenID conformance suite in CI
Dynamic client registration and RP-initiated / front-channel / back-channel logout

use_cases.description: A relying party runs the PKCE authorization code flow with scope=openid and a nonce, exchanges the code at the token endpoint, and receives an ID token alongside the access token.
A relying party verifies an issued ID token's signature against the keys published at the JWKS endpoint.
A relying party presents an access token to the UserInfo endpoint and receives the end-user's scope-derived claims.
A relying party fetches the discovery document to learn the server's OIDC capabilities and endpoint URLs.
A consumer enables OIDC by supplying an asymmetric signing key and OIDC config; a consumer who does not enable OIDC observes identical behavior to today.
A consumer adds custom claims such as roles, tenant, or acr to issued ID tokens via the getIdTokenClaims hook.
A consumer using opaque authorization codes runs the OIDC flow with nonce binding preserved across the authorize-to-token round trip.

decisions: Asymmetric RS256/ES256 signing; one consumer-supplied keypair signs both ID and access tokens when OIDC is enabled; the symmetric HS256 path is unchanged for non-OIDC users (ADR 0001).
No new runtime dependency: jsonwebtoken plus Node's built-in crypto cover signing, JWK export, and thumbprint computation.
The consumer supplies the private key only (PEM string or KeyObject); the library derives the public key and never generates keys at runtime.
v1 ships a single signing key; every token header carries a kid; the JWKS endpoint always publishes a keys array; kid defaults to the RFC 7638 thumbprint and is overridable.

The three OIDC endpoints (UserInfo, discovery, JWKS) are added as methods on AuthorizationServer; ID token issuance lives inside the authorization code grant; there is no separate provider class (ADR 0002).
OIDC is enabled by an explicit oidc config block on AuthorizationServerOptions; an ID token is issued per-request only when the granted scopes include openid.
When OIDC is enabled the library auto-recognizes the standard OIDC scopes (openid, profile, email, address, phone, offline_access) as valid.

A getUserClaims callback supplies user attributes; the default ID token stays lean (protocol claims only); scope-derived claims are served only from UserInfo; the library owns the standard scope-to-claim mapping and filtering.
getUserClaims is keyed by the user identifier, not a loaded user object, so the OIDC config needs no user repository.
The library owns nonce end-to-end; all other OIDC authorization parameters are parsed and exposed to the consumer but not acted on; prompt=none is pass-through.
auth_time is consumer-supplied on the authorization request; the library throws when max_age was requested but auth_time is absent.
Only response_type=code is supported; no implicit or hybrid flows.
Refresh-token issuance is not gated by the library; offline_access handling is left to the consumer's token repository.
The refresh flow stays OIDC-unaware; no ID token is issued on refresh.

Discovery requires explicit endpoint URLs from the consumer; issuer is reused from options and mandatory under OIDC; remaining fields are auto-derived; an optional metadata override is shallow-merged in.
Both opaque and JWT authorization codes are supported under OIDC; nonce and auth_time are added as optional fields on the auth code entity and payload, propagated by both encoders.
JwtInterface gains an optional JWKS-exposing method; OIDC construction throws if the supplied JWT service lacks it.
ID token claims: iss, stringified sub, aud set to the client, exp reusing the access-token TTL, iat, conditional nonce and auth_time, and at_hash; azp is omitted in v1.
An optional getIdTokenClaims hook merges custom claims into the ID token; protocol claims always win and cannot be overridden.
UserInfo requires the openid scope, returns plain JSON, verifies token signature and expiry, and checks revocation when getByAccessToken is implemented.
risks: JwtService.sign currently drops its options argument; it must be fixed to forward algorithm and keyid — a latent bug touched by this work.
Asymmetric signing changes the token format for OIDC consumers (kid header, RS256/ES256); the existing symmetric HS256 path must remain byte-identical so non-OIDC users see no breaking change.
AuthorizationServerOptions is currently flat scalars; nesting an oidc object containing callbacks is a shape change to a public interface — additive but notable.
Consumers using opaque authorization codes must persist two new entity fields (nonce, auth_time) or OIDC nonce binding silently breaks; this requires a clear doc note.
This is a published library — every change must be additive and no public signature may change.
validation: Unit tests: JWK export, RFC 7638 thumbprint kid, RS256/ES256 sign-and-verify round trip, scope-to-claim filtering, discovery document generation, ID token claim assembly, and at_hash computation.
Keystone e2e: a full authorize-to-token flow that verifies the issued ID token's signature against the live JWKS endpoint, then calls UserInfo — run on the vanilla adapter.
e2e: opaque-authorization-code OIDC variant, and a 403 insufficient_scope response when the openid scope is absent at UserInfo.
The existing test suite stays green and the symmetric HS256 path is confirmed unchanged.
Manual smoke test against a real relying-party library (openid-client).
notes: Five implementation phases in dependency order. Phase 1: key service and JWKS — extend JwtService for asymmetric keys, fix sign() to forward options, add a JWKS-exposing method to JwtInterface. Phase 2: the oidc config block on AuthorizationServerOptions plus fail-fast construction guards (issuer set, JWT service exposes JWKS). Phase 3: ID token issuance inside the authorization code grant — nonce and auth_time threaded through the authorization request, auth code entity, payload, and both auth code encoders. Phase 4: the UserInfo endpoint, the claims resolver and standard scope-to-claim mapping, and new invalid_token / insufficient_scope error types. Phase 5: the discovery document, public type exports, and documentation. New code is concentrated under src/oidc/. The framework adapters need no changes — the three new endpoints return a plain ResponseInterface that every adapter already handles. Rough size: 1,300-1,600 lines of library code and 900-1,200 lines of tests. Two ADRs are already recorded: docs/adr/0001 (asymmetric signing) and docs/adr/0002 (OIDC layered on the auth code grant). Domain glossary captured in CONTEXT.md.
